content:
7 min read
Just now

--

The part that makes it click is one boring step past where almost everyone stops.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Artwork by Alan Zenreich (1985)The first few times I used Claude, I thought it was overrated.
I typed in something like “help me plan my week.” What came back was a tidy little list that could have been about anyone’s week. Block time for your priorities. Take breaks. Stay hydrated. It was the kind of advice you would find on a poster in a dentist’s waiting room.
So I did what I think most people do. I decided the hype was not real, closed the tab, and went back to doing it myself.
I was wrong, but not in the way you would expect. The tool was not the problem. The way I was asking was the problem. And the fix was so unglamorous that almost nobody talks about it, which is exactly why almost everybody quits right before the part that actually works.
Quick note before I go on: if you just want the practical version of this, I send one Claude workflow a week, and a free 10-prompt sampler lands in your inbox the moment you join. You can grab it here: https://joshdubowsky.beehiiv.com. Otherwise, here is what I mean.
The wall everybody hits
There is a moment, usually in the first ten minutes, where AI feels like a magic trick that does not quite work on you.
You ask it something. It gives you a confident, generic answer. You think, I could have written that myself, and you are right. So you conclude the thing is shallow and move on.
Here is what is actually happening. You gave it a vague question, so it gave you a vague answer. It had nothing to work with except the average of everything ever written on the topic, so it handed you the average. That is not the ceiling of what it can do. That is the floor. And most people only ever see the floor, because the floor is where they stop.
The people who get real use out of AI are not smarter, and they are not better at some secret skill called prompting. They just went one step further than you did. They told it the specifics.
The one boring step
Compare these two.
The version I quit on:
Help me plan my week.
The version that changed my mind:
Help me plan my week. I have three things I actually need to move forward: finishing a piece of writing that is two days late, sorting out a problem with a bill I have been avoiding, and spending real time with my family on the weekend. My energy is low on weekday mornings and better at night. I get distracted the moment I open my phone. Do not give me a generic schedule. Ask me what is realistic, then build around the low mornings instead of pretending they do not exist.
The first one gets you a poster. The second one gets you something that feels unnervingly like it was written by someone who knows you, because you told it enough to know you.
That is the whole trick. Not clever wording. Not magic phrases. Just the boring, specific truth about your actual situation, typed out instead of assumed. The reason it feels like work is that most of us have never had to say the specifics out loud. We carry them around as a vague fog. AI only gets good when you turn the fog into sentences.
Why the specific version works and the vague one does not
When you say “help me plan my week,” you are asking it to guess. When you say “my energy is low on weekday mornings and I get distracted the moment I open my phone,” you have removed the guessing. It no longer has to write for the average person. It can write for you.
Think about how this works with a real person. If a friend asked you “how do I get fitter” you would give them a shrug and a cliche. If they said “I have a bad knee, I hate gyms, and I have twenty free minutes after I drop the kids at school,” now you can actually help, because they told you what makes their situation theirs. AI is the same. Vague in, vague out. Specific in, useful out.
Almost every complaint I hear about AI being shallow or generic is really a description of a vague question. The person did the vague version, got the floor, and never found out there was a second floor above it.
Three ways to try it tonight
You do not need to learn anything new. You need to add the specifics you were leaving out. Here are three you can copy, paste, and fill in. The square brackets are where your real details go. That filling-in is the entire point, so do not skip it.
The first one, for a decision you keep going back and forth on:
I have a decision I keep going back and forth on and I want to think it through properly.The decision: [WHAT YOU ACTUALLY HAVE TO DECIDE]
The options I am weighing: [LIST THEM]
What I am quietly leaning toward, and why: [BE HONEST]
What I am afraid of getting wrong: [THE REAL FEAR, NOT THE POLITE ONE]Do not tell me what I want to hear. Name the option or the risk I am talking myself out of looking at. Then ask me the one question that would actually change my mind.The second one, for understanding something that has been confusing you:
Explain this to me like I am a capable adult who simply has not learned this yet. No jargon, and if you have to use a technical word, define it in the same sentence.The thing I do not understand: [THE TOPIC, LETTER, FORM, OR IDEA]
What I already sort of know: [WHATEVER YOU HAVE, EVEN IF IT IS LITTLE]
Where I keep getting lost: [THE SPECIFIC PART]Start with the simplest true version. Then check I followed before you add the next layer.The third one, for writing a message you have been avoiding:
Help me write a message I have been putting off.Who it is to: [PERSON AND YOUR RELATIONSHIP TO THEM]
What I need to say: [THE HONEST CORE OF IT]
The tone I want: [WARM, FIRM, APOLOGETIC, WHATEVER IT ACTUALLY IS]
What I am worried it will sound like: [YOUR FEAR ABOUT IT]Give me a version that says the hard part clearly without being cold. Keep it something a real person would actually send.Notice what all three have in common. They tell it the fear, the tone, the part you keep getting lost on. The specifics you would normally keep to yourself are the exact things that turn a generic reply into a useful one.
If these are the kind of thing you want more of, the full library is 200+ prompts built the same way, sorted into 10 categories from money and writing to life admin and the Claude features most people never find. Copy-paste ready, $7 once, no subscription: The Complete Claude Prompt Library. There is a free sample on the page so you can see the format first.
Skip this if
A few honest disclaimers, because this is not for everyone.
Skip this if you already use AI this way. If you are already handing it your real specifics, you found the second floor a long time ago and none of this is new to you.
Skip this if you were hoping for a magic phrase. There is not one. The “trick” is telling the truth about your situation in more detail than feels natural, and that is a habit, not a hack.
Skip this if you want it to run your life for you. It does not do that. It thinks alongside you when you give it something to think about. You are still the one deciding.
Skip this if you came for coding or developer tips. This is about the ordinary chat window, used by someone who does not write software, for the ordinary stuff of a normal week.
The actual takeaway
The gap between “AI is overrated” and “AI is genuinely useful” is not intelligence, and it is not some skill you are missing. It is about four extra sentences of context that you almost typed and then did not.
Most people meet the vague answer, decide that is all there is, and leave. The good part was one boring step away the whole time. It was never a better tool you needed. It was a more honest question.
Try one of the three above tonight. Give it the specifics you would normally keep in your head. See if the thing you gave up on was actually just waiting for you to say more.
The easiest next step is free. Ten prompts in this same copy-paste format, with the same brackets to fill in, land in your inbox the moment you join the newsletter: https://joshdubowsky.beehiiv.com. It is the fastest way to find out whether this way of using AI fits how your week actually works.
